topic: The Eclipse Prediction: Genuine Test or Pre-Fitted?
our_argument_summary: Section 3.5.7 argues that the dome's August 2026 eclipse magnetic prediction (−17 to −21 nT at Ebro) has four structural problems: (1) no mechanism derives the prediction from dome geometry, (2) standard Chapman ionospheric physics already predicts the same 5–30 nT range, (3) two prior eclipse claims (WIN-025 and PROS-003) failed, and (4) the prediction window is wide (±10.5%). The section gives 'credit where due' for the prediction being specific, quantitative, and timestamped.

holes_found.description: The '−17 to −21 nT' prediction range cited in the opening sentence is inaccurate and oversimplified. The dome's actual predictions page (03-predictions.txt) shows a dual-baseline system: BOU baseline gives −5.0 to −12.8 nT across 9 stations, and W004 baseline gives −10.3 to −26.2 nT. Our text says '−17 to −21 nT at Ebro Observatory,' but even Ebro's specific W004 prediction is −21.7 ± 2.1 nT. The BOU prediction for Ebro is only −10.7 nT — squarely within the Chapman range. We are attacking a caricature of the prediction rather than the actual registered prediction. The SEC-3.2 review already flagged this identical problem — it has not been fixed in 3.5.7 either. A dome defender will immediately note we didn't read their predictions page.
holes_found.recommendation: Update the section to accurately describe the dual-baseline prediction structure. The critique is actually STRONGER when you describe both baselines: the BOU baseline predictions (−5 to −13 nT) fall entirely within the Chapman range, making them non-discriminating; the W004 baseline predictions (−10 to −26 nT) extend beyond Chapman's typical range but use an 'empirical recalibration' baseline whose provenance is questionable (see SEC-3.2 review). The combined prediction spans nearly the entire plausible quiet-day eclipse signal space, which is itself a damning observation.

holes_found.description: Section 3.5.7 completely ignores the dome's four model-discriminating tests (E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D). E-PRED-B is particularly important: it predicts Hartland (80% coverage, FSF 1.471) will produce a stronger magnetic anomaly than Ebro (95% coverage, FSF 1.029) despite lower eclipse coverage. This prediction is derived from dome geometry via the FSF = H(r)/r factor — precisely the kind of dome-specific derivation our 'no mechanism' argument claims doesn't exist. By ignoring E-PRED-B, we undermine our own point #1 ('no mechanism shown') because there IS a mechanism shown for relative station ordering. A dome defender will say 'you claim we have no mechanism, but you didn't read E-PRED-B.' The SEC-3.2 review also flagged this gap.
holes_found.recommendation: Address E-PRED-A through E-PRED-D explicitly. For E-PRED-B, acknowledge that the FSF-based relative prediction IS derived from dome geometry, then critique it: (a) The FSF values (0.642–2.075) cannot be independently verified because the H(r) function and its parameters at each station's dome-coordinate location are not published with enough specificity to reproduce the FSF values; (b) Standard ionospheric physics ALSO predicts that eclipse magnetic effects depend on more than just coverage — local Sq current geometry, station latitude, ionospheric conductivity, and local time all affect magnitude, so Hartland > Ebro is plausible under Chapman physics too; (c) With 9 stations and flexible baselines, the model has 18 predictions and only needs a few to 'confirm' to claim success.

holes_found.description: Point #3 states WIN-025 was 'REMOVED because the data was storm-contaminated (Kp 5–6).' This is misleading. According to our own WIN-025 entry in wins.json, the 2024 eclipse study by Chernogor & Holub was conducted under 'quiet geomagnetic conditions (Kp ≤ 3).' The WIN-025 that was removed involved a DIFFERENT eclipse analysis — the dome's own attempt to claim the 2024 data using its storm-contaminated baseline approach. By conflating the quiet-day scientific study with the dome's storm-contaminated analysis, we create confusion about what was actually measured vs. what was claimed. A dome defender could point to the Chernogor & Holub paper showing quiet conditions and accuse us of fabricating the storm contamination claim.
holes_found.recommendation: Clarify the distinction: The April 8, 2024 eclipse DID produce measurable ~10 nT magnetic variations under quiet conditions (Kp ≤ 3), as documented by Chernogor & Holub (2024). What was REMOVED was the dome's WIN-025 claim, which used a different analytical approach involving station data from a day when some stations had elevated activity. Also note that PROS-003's 'scatter −180 to +111 min' timing failure is about the dome's earlier claim that Z-component minima would correlate with eclipse geometry — the timing scatter showed they didn't.

holes_found.description: Point #2 cites 'Curto et al., 2006; Malin et al., 2000' as peer-reviewed literature documenting eclipse magnetic effects, but the reference list at the bottom of the HTML (line 1518-1519) only lists Chapman (1933). These two citations are not in our reference list and their DOIs/full citations are not provided in the section text. A dome defender could claim these are fabricated or unverifiable citations. While both papers are real and well-known in the geomagnetic community (Curto et al. studied eclipse Sq effects; Malin et al. did similar work), not providing DOIs or full references weakens our scholarly rigor.
holes_found.recommendation: Add full references: Curto, J.J., et al. (2006), 'Magnetic effects of the solar eclipse of March 29, 2006,' J. Geophys. Res., 111, A07S13, doi:10.1029/2005JA011561. Malin, S.R.C., et al. (2000), 'The effect of the eclipse on the geomagnetic field,' Ann. Geophysicae, 18, 468–470. Also verify these DOIs resolve correctly — the WIN-008/009/014 precedent shows DOI errors have already been found in this review.

holes_found.description: Point #4 claims the '−17 to −21 nT is a 4 nT range (±10.5%)' with 'multiple chances to claim partial success.' But when you account for the dual-baseline structure, the actual prediction range is much wider: BOU baseline gives −10.7 nT for Ebro, W004 baseline gives −21.7 nT for Ebro. That's a factor of 2× between baselines. Across all 9 stations, the combined BOU+W004 range spans from −5.0 to −26.2 nT — which is essentially the entire physically plausible signal space for a quiet-day eclipse. Our 'wide window' critique dramatically understates how wide the window actually is.
holes_found.recommendation: Replace the ±10.5% calculation with the actual full range: 'The dual-baseline structure spans −5.0 to −26.2 nT across 9 stations. Combined with the ±error bars (up to ±2.6 nT), this covers nearly the entire range of physically plausible quiet-day eclipse magnetic signatures. Any detectable eclipse signal at any station will likely match one baseline.'

holes_found.description: Section 3.5.7's 'credit where due' paragraph is good but insufficient in one respect: it doesn't acknowledge E-PRED-C (SG gravimeters 0.0 μGal prediction). This is actually the dome's most interesting discriminating test — the dome predicts eclipse magnetic effects are electromagnetic (not gravitational), evidenced by SG gravimeters measuring 0.0 μGal. The irony is that this prediction is CORRECT under standard physics too (Van Camp et al. 2001 found 0.0 μGal), and our own WIN-013/014 discuss this. But by not mentioning E-PRED-C, we miss the opportunity to note that the dome's strongest discriminating test is one that standard physics also passes — further reinforcing the non-discriminating nature of the entire eclipse test suite.
holes_found.recommendation: Add a brief note in the 'credit' paragraph or in a new point #5: 'The dome's discriminating test E-PRED-C (SG gravimeters should measure 0.0 μGal) is correct but non-discriminating: Van Camp et al. (2001) already established this result using four superconducting gravimeters during the 1999 eclipse. Standard physics predicts 0.0 μGal because eclipse magnetic effects are ionospheric, not gravitational.'

holes_found.description: The section says 'registered with a git SHA timestamp (2026-03-22)' but the dome's predictions page shows 'Registered 2026-03-12.' Our text says March 22; their text says March 12. A 10-day discrepancy on the registration date is a minor factual error but one a dome defender could use to question our attention to detail.
holes_found.recommendation: Verify the registration date. If the git SHA timestamp is from March 22 but the page text says March 12, both could be correct (page update vs. git commit). If our date is wrong, correct to 2026-03-12. If different dates reflect different events, clarify which date refers to what.

holes_found.description: The section says 'seven stations' but the dome's predictions page lists NINE stations (E001–E009: Ebro, San Pablo, Eskdalemuir, Lerwick, Hartland, Niemegk, Canary Islands, Chambon, Coimbra). Saying 'seven' when there are nine is a factual error that, while minor, reinforces a dome defender's claim that we didn't read the prediction carefully.

stronger_arguments.description: The dual-baseline hedging strategy deserves explicit analysis. The BOU baseline (−10.9 nT from 2017) produces predictions in the −5 to −13 nT range — entirely within Chapman's 5–20 nT range. The W004 baseline (−22.24 nT, 'empirical recalibration' from V50.2) produces predictions in the −10 to −26 nT range. Together they span −5 to −26 nT. If the observation is −10 nT, the BOU baseline 'confirms.' If it's −22 nT, the W004 baseline 'confirms.' The only failing outcome would be an observation near 0 nT — which the Kp < 2 escape clause excludes. The prediction is engineered to be nearly unfalsifiable.

The W004 baseline provenance is a major vulnerability the dome's prediction rests on. According to evolution page entries, W004 was an 'empirical recalibration' added in V50.2 — originally a weekly test result near or below the detection threshold that was repurposed as a baseline. The dome's stronger prediction set (the one that extends beyond Chapman's range) is built on a data point the dome itself previously classified differently. This provenance chain undermines the stronger of the two baselines.

The 'pre-fitted vs genuine' question in the section title has a clear answer that the section doesn't state explicitly enough: the prediction IS pre-fitted. The formula delta_Z = baseline × coverage × FSF takes a globe-era INTERMAGNET observation (−10.9 nT) as its baseline, eclipse coverage percentages derived from standard Besselian element ephemeris calculations (which assume spherical Earth geometry), and an FSF factor whose derivation from dome geometry is not independently verifiable. Every input to the dome's eclipse prediction comes from standard physics data. The dome's contribution is the FSF scaling factor — but without a published, reproducible derivation of FSF from dome parameters, it could be a free parameter chosen to produce reasonable-looking results.

There is a quantitative inconsistency between the published prediction and the monitoring code that our Section 5.16 mentions but Section 3.5.7 does not. The homepage prediction for Ebro is −21.7 nT (W004 baseline). But monitor.py computes ecm_eclipse = −18.22 × 0.95 × 1.672 ≈ −29.0 nT. The monitoring system will test −29 nT while the page advertises −21.7 nT — a 34% discrepancy. Which prediction is the dome actually committed to? This internal inconsistency should be noted in 3.5.7 as a fifth structural problem.

kernel_of_truth.description: The eclipse prediction genuinely represents the dome model's best attempt at prospective, falsifiable science. It provides per-station predictions with specific error bars, dual baselines, four discriminating sub-tests, and pre-registration via Git + OpenTimestamps. E-PRED-B (Hartland > Ebro despite lower coverage) is a genuinely interesting relative prediction that goes beyond 'the signal will exist.' The prediction is structurally more sophisticated than anything else in the 67-WIN portfolio. Our 'credit where due' paragraph is right to acknowledge this.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Sophistication of structure is not the same as independence of derivation. Every numerical input to the prediction comes from standard physics data sources: INTERMAGNET measurements, Besselian ephemeris calculations, and an FSF factor that cannot be independently verified from published dome parameters. The prediction is a dome-labeled processing of globe-sourced data. The dual-baseline hedging ensures nearly any quiet-day result will match one prediction. And the Kp < 2 precondition means ~60% of possible eclipse conditions are automatically excluded. The prediction is designed to be very difficult to fail — which is not what 'most falsifiable' means.

text_fingerprint.section_title: The Eclipse Prediction: Genuine Test or Pre-Fitted?

deeper_analysis:
Section 3.5.7 is strategically important because it answers the central question a reader will have about the dome's eclipse prediction: is this a real test? The answer is 'partially' — the structure is real, but the construction ensures near-unfalsifiability.

However, the section as written has significant overlap problems with Section 3.2 and Section 6.2, which also analyze the eclipse prediction. The three sections should either be consolidated or clearly differentiated. Currently:
- Section 3.2 focuses on the 'misrepresented prediction' framing (false binary with globe)
- Section 3.5.7 focuses on 'genuine test or pre-fitted' (structural problems)
- Section 6.2 provides a final summary of the eclipse test

The overlap means that errors in one section (like the wrong prediction range) propagate to all three, and a reader who reads all three may feel the review is padding rather than building new argument.

The biggest strategic weakness across all three eclipse sections is the failure to address E-PRED-B. This is the dome's single most interesting prediction — a RELATIVE ordering claim that doesn't depend on absolute baselines. If the August 2026 eclipse shows Hartland > Ebro in magnetic anomaly magnitude despite lower coverage, the dome will claim a genuine dome-derived prediction was confirmed. Our review currently has no counter-argument ready for this scenario. We should either:
(a) Show that standard Sq current physics can also produce Hartland > Ebro (via latitude, local time, and ionospheric conductivity differences), or
(b) Acknowledge it as a genuinely discriminating sub-test while noting it's embedded in a larger prediction framework that hedges the absolute magnitudes.

The '−17 to −21 nT' error is particularly damaging because it appears in all three eclipse sections (3.2, 3.5.7, and 6.2), giving the impression of a systematic failure to read the dome's actual predictions page. Fixing this in all three places should be a high priority.

On the other hand, thinking as a dome defender would: the section's strongest point is #1 (no mechanism). Even though E-PRED-B uses FSF from H(r)/r, the FSF values themselves are not derivable by a third party because the H(r) function parameters at each station's dome-coordinate position are not published with sufficient precision. A genuine prediction from dome geometry would be: here are the dome parameters, here is the H(r) function, here is how to compute FSF at each station's dome coordinates — verify for yourself. The dome provides none of this. The FSF values are stated, not derived in a reproducible way.

citations_verified: Chapman (1933) — standard attribution in geomagnetism, pre-DOI era, universally cited as Chapman 1933 or Chapman & Bartels 1940
citations_unchecked: Curto et al. (2006) — cited in passing without DOI or full reference; likely J. Geophys. Res. 111, A07S13, doi:10.1029/2005JA011561 but not verified
Malin et al. (2000) — cited in passing without DOI or full reference; likely Ann. Geophysicae 18, 468-470 but not verified
git SHA timestamp date '2026-03-22' — dome's page says 'Registered 2026-03-12'; discrepancy unresolved
